architectureFigure.caption: Example architecture of a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) system.

authors: Teri Rumble, Zbyněk Gazdík, Javad Zarrin, and Jagdeep Ahluwalia

breakthroughAssessment.justification: This paper makes a strong contribution by addressing a practical and challenging problem: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) over structured knowledge graphs in e-commerce. It provides a systematic evaluation of various retriever-reranker pipeline components and introduces specific well-engineered pipeline variants that significantly outperform existing benchmarks. The empirical validation on a large-scale, proprietary-like dataset (Amazon STaRK SKB) provides strong evidence of its utility and offers actionable insights for real-world deployments. While the core RAG components exist, the novel combination, optimization, and rigorous benchmarking for this specific, complex domain (SKB + cross-encoders) constitute a substantial and impactful step forward, moving beyond general RAG to domain-specific, production-ready systems.

coreProblem.statement: Applying Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to structured knowledge graphs (KGs), especially in e-commerce, presents challenges in scaling retrieval across connected graphs, preserving contextual relationships during response generation, and effectively integrating cross-encoders with structured data.
whyItMatters: Conventional Large Language Models (LLMs) are prone to generating hallucinated or outdated content, especially when queries relate to proprietary or non-public information.
RAG addresses this limitation by grounding LLM responses in authoritative, domain-specific external knowledge, which is crucial for enterprise applications.
Production-level RAG systems for complex data structures like knowledge graphs require semantic reasoning capabilities, fast execution, and low computational costs to be scalable.
E-commerce platforms present unique challenges due to their scale, proprietary nature, complex structural organization of product catalogs, and diverse, complex customer queries.
coreProblem.concreteExample: Processing natural language queries for product information from large-scale, proprietary e-commerce product catalogs (Knowledge Bases) with millions of products and complex relationships.

evaluationHighlights: Achieved 20.4% higher Hit@1 and 14.5% higher Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR) compared to published benchmarks on the Amazon STaRK dataset, demonstrating significant performance gains.
Empirically demonstrated that cross-encoder reranking provides statistically significant improvements over standalone retrieval methods, substantially boosting overall pipeline effectiveness.
Identified the FAISS HNSW + webis/set-encoder-large pipeline configuration as the best-performing model, excelling across all evaluation metrics.
Validated that careful model selection, optimized pre-processing, and multi-stage pipeline design are critical for building scalable, enterprise-grade RAG systems tailored for e-commerce.

benchmarks: STaRK Synthesized Prompt and Response Key dataset (9,100 natural language queries)
evaluationSetup.setting: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipelines for e-commerce natural language queries over semi-structured knowledge bases, focusing on retrieval and reranking performance prior to text generation.

experimentFigures.caption: Text Pre-processing Method Comparison with 100 queries.

Performance Comparison of Retrieval Models Across Key Metrics. Four-panel visualization showing performance distributions for three retrieval approaches across Hit@1, Hit@5, Recall@20, and MRR metrics (panels A–D, respectively). Vertical error bars represent 95% confidence intervals around mean performance scores. Evaluation conducted on validation dataset (n=910 queries).

Performance Comparison of Re-ranking Models Across Key Metrics. Four-panel visualization showing performance distributions for six re-ranking approaches across Hit@1, Hit@5, Recall@20, and MRR metrics (panels A–D, respectively). Vertical error bars represent 95% confidence intervals around mean performance scores. Evaluation conducted on validation dataset (n=910 queries).

mainTakeaways: Cross-encoder reranking consistently provides statistically significant improvements over standalone retrieval methods across all models and metrics.

The webis/set-encoder-large cross-encoder, particularly when combined with FAISS HNSW retrieval, achieved the highest performance across all evaluation metrics, demonstrating its superiority in fine-grained relevance ranking.
Dense retrieval methods (FAISS-based) generally demonstrated stronger performance for Recall@20 compared to sparse retrieval (BM25), indicating better coverage of relevant items.
The optimized Retriever-Reranker pipelines developed in this study significantly exceed the performance of published benchmarks on the Amazon STaRK dataset.

Model selection is crucial; NLI-based and sentence similarity cross-encoders significantly underperformed compared to other re-ranking architectures, highlighting the importance of task-specific model choice.

keyNovelty.main: Design and empirical evaluation of specialized Retriever-Reranker pipelines for Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) over proprietary semi-structured knowledge bases (SKBs) in e-commerce, integrating dense/sparse retrieval with cross-encoder reranking.
explanation: The paper introduces and technically specifies three novel Retriever-Reranker pipeline configurations: FRWSR (FAISS Retrieval + Webis Set-encoder Reranking), FRMR (FAISS Retrieval + MS MARCO Reranking), and BARMR (BM25 Augmented Retrieval + MS MARCO Reranking). These pipelines integrate dense or sparse retrieval with advanced cross-encoder reranking specifically for knowledge graphs.

It conducts the first empirical performance evaluation of these specialized RAG pipelines on the Amazon STaRK benchmark, a production-scale e-commerce dataset that includes detailed product attributes and inter-product relationships. This evaluation uses ground-truth node responses, enabling direct comparison with published baselines.

The study provides actionable insights for optimizing RAG models that leverage Semi-structured Knowledge Base (SKB) architectures in natural language query processing. It demonstrates substantial performance improvements (e.g., 20.4% higher Hit@1 and 14.5% higher MRR) over existing benchmarks, informing the development of scalable, enterprise-grade RAG systems for domain-specific applications.

keyTerms.definition: A framework that combines a pre-trained generative language model with an external knowledge retriever to ground responses in factual, up-to-date information, thereby mitigating hallucination.

A type of neural re-ranking model that jointly encodes a query and a document as a single input, allowing for full self-attention across all tokens to capture fine-grained semantic interactions and output a relevance score.
A knowledge repository that combines elements of structured data (e.g., product attributes) with less rigidly structured text (e.g., reviews, descriptions), often represented as a graph.
An evaluation metric that assesses whether a relevant document appears within the top-$k$ positions of the reranked list.
An evaluation metric that measures the proportion of relevant items in the top-$k$ prediction results, indicating the model's ability to identify all relevant items.
An evaluation metric that calculates the average inverse rank of the first relevant document across queries, emphasizing the rank of the first correct answer.

prerequisites: Large Language Models (LLMs) and their architectures (e.g., Transformers)
Information Retrieval (IR) concepts, including sparse and dense retrieval methods
Knowledge Graphs (KGs) and graph data structures
Vector embeddings and similarity search algorithms (e.g., FAISS, HNSW)

comparisonToPriorWork: Unlike conventional RAG systems that perform well on unstructured text, this study specifically addresses the challenges of applying RAG to complex structured knowledge graphs, such as those found in e-commerce product catalogs, which often involve multi-hop reasoning and diverse query types.

The paper provides a systematic evaluation framework to overcome limitations identified in previous work on the STaRK dataset, which struggled with sufficient processing of edge data within the knowledge graph.

While Large Language Models (LLMs) can be used for re-ranking, this work emphasizes cross-encoders as a more computationally efficient and often more consistent alternative for the specific task of re-ranking query-document pairs, especially in production environments, compared to the context window limitations and processing costs of general-purpose LLMs.

The study evaluates different types of cross-encoders, noting that point-wise and set-wise methods are more feasible for large text contexts compared to computationally intensive pair-wise and list-wise models.
limitations: The use of general-purpose LLMs for re-ranking can be impractical in production due to context window limits and significant processing times and costs.
Pair-wise and list-wise cross-encoder models are often infeasible for large texts due to memory input token constraints, limiting the study's focus to point-wise and set-wise approaches.
BM25 retrieval methods, while efficient, lack the ability to capture deeper semantic relationships between terms, relying on exact or near-exact keyword matches.
Dense retrieval methods (e.g., FAISS) can be computationally expensive for large-scale indices and their performance relies heavily on the quality of embeddings.

modeling.label: Retrieval Strategies
list: BM25: A sparse lexical retrieval method, efficient and interpretable, but limited to exact term matching and lacks capture of semantic similarity.
BM25 Augmented: BM25 enhanced with 1-hop knowledge graph expansion to improve lexical matches, though with increased computational overhead.
FAISS - FLAT: A dense retrieval method (vector-based, exact search) using E5-Large embeddings, offering brute-force nearest neighbor search but is computationally expensive for large-scale indices.

FAISS - HNSW: A dense retrieval method (vector-based, approximate search) using Hierarchical Navigable Small World (HNSW) algorithm for rapid semantic search through graph-based hops, with slight reduction in accuracy due to approximation.

modeling.label: Reranking Models (Cross-encoders & LLMs)
list: ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2: A point-wise cross-encoder for passage ranking, distilled MiniLM with 6 layers, optimized for speed and low latency.
ms-marco-MiniLM-L-12-v2: A point-wise cross-encoder for passage ranking, a 12-layer version offering better accuracy.
BAAI/bge-reranker-base: A point-wise BERT-based reranker trained with list-wise contrastive learning for dense retrieval and reranking.
webis/set-encoder-base/large: Set-wise permutation-invariant attention transformer encoders designed for novelty detection and modeling long-range dependencies across documents.
Claude 4 Sonnet: An LLM-based re-ranker, selected for its recent release, robust API, and substantial token capacity for processing longer document contexts.

modeling.label: Embedding Model
modeling.value: E5-Large embedding model used for dense retrieval operations.

pipelineFlow: Data Modification: Semi-structured knowledge source undergoes data cleaning and metadata enrichment.
Indexing: Processed data is fed into an indexing component to construct and maintain embedded representations in a vector database.
Query Preprocessing: User natural language queries undergo tokenization, normalization, or expansion.
Retrieval: Manipulated query is compared against the indexed vector store (via semantic or lexical search) to retrieve a candidate set of 'top k' entries.
Reranking: The candidate set is passed to a re-ranking module (e.g., cross-encoder) that refines their order by computing fine-grained relevance scores.
Generation: The reordered results are provided to a natural language generator (e.g., LLM) to produce a contextually relevant response to the query.

problemDefinition.label: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
problemDefinition.value: Formally, given a query $q \in Q$ and a large corpus $D$, the goal is to generate an output $a \in A$ that is factually consistent with respect to $D$. RAG explicitly decomposes this problem into retrieval, reranking, and generation.

problemDefinition.label: Retriever Function
problemDefinition.value: A retriever $R$ maps the query $q$ to a candidate set of $k$ documents: $C_k(q) = R(q) \subset D$, with $|C_k(q)| = k$.

problemDefinition.label: KG-augmented Retriever
problemDefinition.value: The retriever $R_G$ extends conventional retrieval by an optional structured expansion step, augmenting the candidate set $C_k(q)$ with 1-hop neighbors from the knowledge graph $G$: $C_k^G(q) = R_G(q) = \text{Expand}_{G,1}(C_k(q)) = C_k(q) \cup \bigcup_{v \in \eta(C_k(q))} N_G^{(1)}(v)$.

problemDefinition.label: Reranker Function
problemDefinition.value: A reranker $f$ assigns fine-grained scores to candidates $d \in C_k(q)$, inducing a permutation $\pi_f: C_k(q) \to \{1, ..., k\}$, where $d_j$ denotes the document with $\pi_f(d_j)=j$.

problemDefinition.label: Generator Function
problemDefinition.value: A generator $G$ conditions on the query $q$ and the reranked set $C_k(q)$ to produce an answer: $a = G(q, \pi_f(C_k(q)))$.

technicalDetails.reproducibility: The data used in this study (Amazon STaRK SKB and Synthesized Prompt and Response Key) are publicly available from the Hugging Face repository snap-stanford/stark. The code associated with all three proposed Retriever-Reranker pipelines is available in a public Github repository (https://github.com/tep00018/ECommerceRAG).

thesis: This study designs and comparatively evaluates multiple Retriever-Reranker pipelines, integrating dense or sparse retrieval with cross-encoder reranking, specifically optimized for natural language queries over proprietary semi-structured knowledge bases (SKBs) in e-commerce, demonstrating significant performance improvements over existing benchmarks.
title: Comparative Analysis of Neural Retriever-Reranker Pipelines for Retrieval-Augmented Generation over Knowledge Graphs in E-commerce Applications
